poses the supervised learning lifecycle into 7 kernel primitives connected
by a typed directed acyclic graph (DAG), with four hard constraints that
reject the two most damaging leakage classes at call time. Invalid compositions within the grammar's seven primitives are rejected by type checks
or call-time guards. For compliant usage, detection becomes unnecessary. The grammar's core contribution is the terminal assess constraint: a[cs.LG]
runtime-enforced evaluate/assess boundary where repeated test-set assessment is rejected by a guard on a nominally distinct Evidence type, making
test-set reuse rejected at call time within the grammar's API.

text:
My companion study across 2,047 experimental instances (Roth 2026, in preparation) quantifies why this matters: selection leakage inflates performance
by 𝑑𝑧= 0.93 (raw AUC inflation: +0.046 points, 95% CI [0.043, 0.048])
and memorization leakage by 𝑑𝑧= 0.53–1.11, scaling with model capacity, while estimation leakage is negligible (|𝑑| < 0.1). A follow-up study
across 3,759 additional instances establishes that Class II effects persist
at all tested sample sizes: a power law fit produces a positive asymptotic floor of 𝑑∞= 0.047 (𝑁= 493 datasets), confirming that the grammar's unconditional rejection is empirically necessary within the tested
range (𝑛= 50–2,000). The grammar generated three falsifiable predictions before observing experimental results. Two confirmed, one falsified. The grammar survives empirical test. Three separate implementations
(Python, R, and Julia) confirm the claims. The appendix specificationarXiv:2603.10742v1
lets anyone build a conforming version. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 Kapoor and Narayanan (2023) audited the machine learning literature and
found data leakage errors in 294 published papers across 17 scientific fields. This is not a knowledge gap: Kaufman et al. (2012) formalized the leakage
taxonomy a decade earlier. Cawley and Talbot (2010) demonstrated selection
bias in model evaluation fifteen years ago. The problem persists because documentation does not prevent these failures. It affects 17.5% of neuroimaging
studies (Rosenblatt et al. 2024) and propagates into field-level meta-analytic
conclusions (van de Mortel and van Wingen 2025). The response to this crisis has been documentation: best-practice guides, checklists, linting tools.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Yang, Breck, and Whang (2022) built LeakageDetector,
achieving 92.9% accuracy on three leakage types, demonstrating that detection
is feasible. A natural follow-up question is whether prevention is also possible:
can leakage be made to fail at call time by the composition rules of the workflow
itself, rather than detected after the fact? This paper addresses that question
for tabular supervised learning — binary classification, multiclass classification,
and regression — on the three leakage classes for which effect sizes have been
empirically quantified. Temporal leakage, target leakage, and distribution mismatch are real problems the grammar does not currently address; extending
the type DAG to cover them requires empirical baselines analogous to those
established for Classes I–III. The history of formal grammars suggests it can. In every domain where practitioners composed complex artifacts from simpler parts, a grammar eventually
emerged that separated valid from invalid compositions: Year Author Domain Primitives 1957 Chomsky Language Phrase rules + transformations
1967 Bertin Cartography 7 retinal variables
1970 Codd Databases 8 relational operators
1999 Wilkinson Statistical graphics Data, aesthetics, geoms, scales,
coords, facets
2010 Wickham Statistical graphics Layers, defaults, embedding in R
(impl.) All five entries share a common move: enumerating the primitives of a domain. But they differ on composition. Bertin catalogued visual channels by perceptual capacity: a taxonomy that identified primitives without defining rules for
combining them or criteria for rejecting invalid combinations. Chomsky, Codd,
Wilkinson, and Wickham went further: each defined composition rules and a
rejection criterion that makes "this is wrong" a formal statement rather than an
opinion. "Grammar" here follows Chomsky rather than Wilkinson: a finite rule
system that partitions sequences into well-formed and ill-formed, not a combinatorial algebra of independently composable layers. The grammars that survived A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 had executable implementations: SQL for Codd, ggplot2 for Wickham, parsers
for Chomsky.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Without one, a grammar stays academic. Wickham (2010)'s contribution was not the API; it
was the successful implementation and selection. ggplot2 is not a complete
implementation of Wilkinson's full system; it exposes a curated subset of it. By choosing which primitives to make primary and which to omit, Wickham
shifted which errors were easy to make: incomplete specifications fail at construction, and correct compositions become the default path. The analogous
selection has not been made for the ML lifecycle.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Kuhn and Silge (2022) made
the most important prior contribution: recipes inside workflows enforce per-fold
preprocessing, structurally preventing Class I leakage. The remaining question
is whether the type system can go one level further — distinguishing "this data
has been split correctly" from "this data has not," and marking a model as
terminally assessed. tidymodels types serve implementation correctness (recipe,
parsnip model, workflow); the grammar adds a methodological correctness layer
on top (Partition, PreparedData, Model with assessed state). This paper makes that selection for supervised learning. 2 A Motivating Example Consider a binary classification task. The standard workflow in scikit-learn
(Pedregosa et al. 2011): # Standard sklearn — Pipeline fixes this; the grammar goes further
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X) # Leaks: sees test rows
scores = cross_val_score(LogisticRegression(), X_scaled, y, cv=5)
print(f"CV AUC: {scores.mean():.3f}") # Optimistic The scaler sees all rows before the cross-validation split. This is Class I leakage
(estimation bias). Its effect is negligible (𝑑≈0; Roth (2026)).
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The canonical
sklearn fix (wrapping scaler and classifier in a Pipeline) solves this case cleanly
by keeping preprocessing inside the cross-validation loop. The grammar builds
on that insight and extends it to the next boundary: distinguishing evaluation
from assessment, enforcing assess-once, and addressing the more damaging Class
II (𝑑𝑧= 0.93) and Class III (𝑑𝑧= 0.53–1.11) patterns that operate above the
preprocessing level. The normalization example is used here because it is structurally visible; the grammar's contribution starts where Pipeline's protection
ends. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 s = ml.split(df, target="y", seed=42)
model = ml.fit(s.train, "y", algorithm="rf", seed=42)
metrics = ml.evaluate(model, s.valid)
final = ml.assess(model, test=s.test) Each line produces a typed output that constrains what the next line can accept.
fit requires a Partition's training fold, not raw data. assess requires a Model
that has not been previously assessed. Preparation happens inside fit, per fold,
by default.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The leaky workflow is rejected: scaler.fit_transform(X) on the
full dataset produces a DataFrame that was never registered by split. When
that result is passed to fit, the provenance guard rejects it: the data has no
split provenance. The grammar rejects it at call time, not after the fact.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text: 3.1 Kernel primitives The grammar decomposes 36 API verbs into 7 kernel primitives: # Primitive Type signature What it does
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
1 split DataFrame →Partition Establish assessment boundary and
iteration protocol
2 prepare DataFrame → Normalize, encode, impute features
PreparedData
3 fit DataFrame × target → Train a model
Model
4 predict Model × DataFrame → Apply model to new data
Predictions
5 evaluate Model × DataFrame → Measure on validation data
Metrics (repeatable)
6 explain Model [× DataFrame] → Feature importances, partial
Explanation dependence
7 assess Model × DataFrame → Measure on test data (terminal,
Evidence once) assess returns Evidence: a nominally distinct type with no downstream consumers. No primitive accepts Evidence as input. It flows to the human, not
to the next step. explain is similarly terminal: its output feeds no further
primitive. explain is the grammar's unconstrained primitive. Every other
kernel primitive carries at least one partition guard: split accepts any A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 DataFrame; prepare and fit require registered {train, valid, dev}-tagged
data; evaluate rejects test-tagged and unregistered data. assess requires
test-tagged data and fires once per model. explain requires only a fitted
Model. It may be called on any data, any number of times, before or after
assess.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Explanation is diagnostic, not part of the validity chain. explain
is outside the grammar's rejection criterion by design: the grammar has no
partition guard to place on it. The assess-once constraint is enforced by the call-time guard model.assessed
= false, which fires before the call completes. A second call assess(model,
test=s.test) still passes the type check (the model argument is still a Model)
but fails the guard, which then raises. This is a guard rejection, not a type
error. It requires runtime state and cannot be caught by a type-checker before
the call executes. This distinction is discussed in Section 4. Declarative default: fit accepts a tagged DataFrame (partition_tag in
{train, valid}) and handles preparation internally, per fold. In explicit mode,
the user calls ml.prepare() first, receiving a PreparedData object; fit then
accepts PreparedData as input instead. Both modes satisfy the same guards.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The correct workflow should be the obvious choice; calling prepare explicitly is
available but never required. Why fit accepts valid-tagged data. The {train, valid} guard allows
refitting on combined train+validation data after hyperparameter selection is
complete, before the final terminal assessment. This is the dev = train +
valid workflow. It is not a guard relaxation; it is a recognized final-preparation
step before assess.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The iterative/final fit distinction. The type signature fit :: DataFrame
× target →Model is identical whether fit is called during the iterate zone
(training a candidate model per fold, evaluated and discarded) or as the final
fit(dev) call after hyperparameters are fixed. These two roles differ in commitment: per-fold models are expendable. The final fit(dev) model is the
one that proceeds to assess.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The grammar assigns both calls the same type
because they satisfy the same preconditions. The semantic distinction (which
fit is final) is invisible to the type system. A practitioner who treats a per-fold
model as the committed model makes a semantic error, not a structural one. The diagnostics layer (check, report) catches it. The type DAG does not. Seven is empirical, not derived — the smallest set of operations practitioners actually perform that covers them without collapsing a type
boundary or losing a postcondition.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The clearest case: predict and evaluate
both consume a Model, but they carry different guards. predict imposes no
partition constraint (it is valid on any data, including untagged deployment
data), while evaluate requires partition_tag = valid. Merging them forces
a choice: adopt the stricter guard and break the deploy use case, or adopt the
weaker guard and allow evaluation on untagged or training data. Either way, a
guard that exists only because the primitives are separate collapses.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 argument applies to each: split alone produces a Partition. prepare alone
fits and returns a reusable Transformer state (absorbing it into fit destroys
the ability to apply consistent preprocessing to held-out data without refitting).
assess alone produces Evidence. Removing any one loses either a type or a
guard that the rejection criterion depends on.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The primitives connect through a typed directed acyclic graph (Figure 1). Reading top to bottom: a single DataFrame enters a split decision (diamond), which
routes data into three named partitions — train, valid, and test. The train
and valid partitions feed a fit/evaluate loop (the cycle on the left of the diagram) where the practitioner iterates freely: fitting models, inspecting validation metrics, adjusting hyperparameters. The test partition, by contrast, is
gated (marked ×): it remains locked until the practitioner calls assess exactly
once, producing terminal evidence that the grammar does not allow to be revisited. The visual asymmetry is the point; iteration is cheap and encouraged,
commitment is expensive and irreversible. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
permitted locked / once ✕ gate iterate terminal output Figure 1: The ML workflow grammar as a typed DAG. Diamond: split decision. Solid arrows: permitted data flow. Dashed arrows: locked until terminal assessment. Gate (×): test partition held until assess is called once, producing terminal evidence. The fit–evaluate loop (grayed out) iterates freely on train/valid
partitions; assess commits on the held-out test partition exactly once. The Partition type has two variants: Variant Interface Assessment boundary Holdout .train, .valid, .test .test held out
CV .folds, .k Test provided at assess(test=) The split primitive accepts awareness parameters that compose with both variants: stratify=True (class balance, default), groups="patient_id" (group
membership), time="date" (temporal order). Every awareness parameter addresses a specific leakage mechanism.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
split is the most parameterized primitive. split(groups="patient_id")
and split(time="date") produce Partitions with qualitatively different validity properties: GroupKFold forbids group overlap across folds. PurgedKFold
additionally enforces temporal ordering and an embargo gap. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 separate primitives — they are domain specializations of one primitive. All variants share the type signature DataFrame →Partition. What differs
is the guard system. split_temporal carries guards that split does not:
embargo enforcement, expanding-vs-sliding window policy, temporal ordering
with no future leakage. split_group carries a group-non-overlap guard that is
meaningless for random splits. The specializations are constraint profiles, not
type distinctions: each conjugates the same grammatical verb for a different
scientific domain (time series forecasting, clinical trials with repeated measures,
standard cross-sectional ML). The primitive count remains 7.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The implementation exposes split, split_temporal, and split_group as independent entry
points, each with domain-specific parameters and error messages, rather than
burying the domain logic behind parameter combinations on a single function. This is a design choice: the grammar's type system sees one primitive; the API
surface reflects that different fields require different structural constraints on
the same partitioning operation. 3.3 The four hard constraints # Constraint What it prevents Leakage class 1 Assess once per Repeated test-set peeking Class II (𝑑𝑧= 0.93)
model
2 Prepare after Global preprocessing leakage Class I (𝑑≈0)
split, per fold
3 Type-safe Fitting on test/untagged data; Class II/III
transitions evaluating without a fitted model (𝑑𝑧= 0.53–1.11)
4 No label access Feature selection using test labels Class II (𝑑𝑧= 0.93)
before split The constraints are empirically grounded. In the companion study (Roth 2026),
Class II effects (𝑑𝑧= 0.93, raw AUC inflation +0.046) and Class III effects
(𝑑𝑧= 0.53–1.11) are large enough to invalidate published results. A followup study across 493 datasets at six sample sizes (3,759 experimental instances
total) confirms that Class II effects persist with a positive asymptotic floor
(𝑑∞= 0.047), establishing that the grammar's rejection is empirically necessary
within the tested range. Class I effects are negligible (𝑑≈0), but Constraint 2 is
retained because it is principled (consistent with all textbook recommendations)
and costless (per-fold preparation is the default). The constraints are also minimal: 4 rules, not 40. Chomsky's programme stalled
when syntactic rules proliferated to cover semantic territory. Codd's relational
model succeeded by stating a small number of inviolable properties and leaving
optimization to implementations. More rules do not necessarily mean better
protection.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 3.4 Strategy families The 7 primitives compose into 4 strategy families covering the full developmental
workflow: Family Question Strategies
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Selection Which algorithm? screen, compare, pick
Optimization Which hyperparameters? tune (grid, random, bayesian)
Evaluation How to rotate data? kfold, grouped, purged, nested_cv
Ensemble How to combine models? blend, bag, boost, stack The strategies decompose into kernel primitives: • screen: ∀algo: fit →evaluate →rank →Leaderboard
• tune: ∀params: fit →evaluate →select_best →TuningResult
• stack: ∀model: fit →out-of-fold predict →fit_meta →StackedModel No strategy requires a new primitive: each reduces to typed applications of the
7 kernel verbs. That the core developmental workflow fits within this decomposition (for the strategies examined here) is evidence that the primitives capture
structure, not an accidental API boundary. The output containers Leaderboard,
TuningResult, and StackedModel are informal wrappers for composition results;
they are convenience wrappers, not part of the workflow grammar itself. The erased intermediate in tune. The tune decomposition hides a type the
grammar does not declare: after select_best identifies optimal parameters,
those parameters — a HyperParameters intermediate — feed the final fit(dev)
call. The grammar's visible type chain is TuningResult →fit →Model; the
HyperParameters intermediate exists in every implementation but is not a sort
in the type DAG. This is a deliberate compression: tune is a strategy, not a
primitive, and its internal types are outside the grammar's minimal vocabulary. A conforming extension could expose HyperParameters as a declared sort with
its own typed accessor from TuningResult, making the refitting step explicit
and enabling a guard that rejects fit(dev) called without a completed tune
cycle. The constraints and composition rules generate
testable predictions: if a strategy violates a constraint, measurable inflation
should follow. Three were tested in the companion study before observing V2
results:

title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The full experimental results are in Section 7.3. 3.5 The terminal boundary The evaluate/assess distinction is the grammar's central structural claim. • Evaluate operates on validation data. It is repeatable, cheap, and safe for
decisions. It informs the iterate cycle: the practitioner reads the Metrics
output, adjusts, and fits again. Metrics is terminal in the type system (no
primitive accepts it as input), but it drives iteration at the practitioner
level.
• Assess operates on test data. It is terminal, once per model, and irreversible. It is a deliberate commit: the model's performance on data it
has never influenced. The distinction encodes the textbook structure of model assessment (Hastie,
Tibshirani, and Friedman 2009, chap. 7). Generalization error (§7.1) becomes
assess producing Evidence: frozen, terminal, unrevisitable. Training error is
available internally but not reported.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Model assessment (§7.2) is evaluate on
validation, repeatable by design. Model selection maps to the Selection and
Optimization families. Cross-validation (§7.10) is split(folds=k) feeding the
Evaluation family. The two textbook failure modes (preprocessing before splitting at §7.10.2, "the wrong way"; reusing the test set at §7.7) map directly to
Constraints 4, 2, and 1. A student who learns ML with this grammar internalizes the separation from day one. The evaluate/assess distinction, explained in
every textbook and violated in 294 papers, is enforced by the types, not by the
student's memory. The three properties below are design constraints, not theorems. Codd's data
independence and Wickham's layer independence play the same role in their
grammars. Any proposed extension can be validated against them. So can any
third-party implementation. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 The word "grammar" here follows Wilkinson and Wickham, not Chomsky. A
Chomsky grammar generates strings by derivation rules; this grammar generates
typed workflows enforced by the type DAG and call-time guards.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The two senses
are not equivalent, but the term is retained because it is the established name
in the Wilkinson–Wickham tradition that this work extends. The grammar uses two kinds of rejection. Structural rejection (type mismatch)
is static: a function that accepts Model cannot receive a Partition. Guard rejection is dynamic: it requires runtime state (e.g., model.assessed = false)
and fires before the call completes. The four hard constraints use both mechanisms: assess-once is a guard; the type DAG prevents nonsensical type chains
structurally. The workflow's valid state transitions form a regular language
recognizable by a 4-state deterministic finite automaton (DFA) with states
{CREATED, FITTED, EVALUATED, ASSESSED}.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
ASSESSED is a terminal
sink that rejects all further transitions. The primary path is FITTED →
EVALUATED →ASSESSED, but a shortcut FITTED →ASSESSED is valid
(assessing without prior evaluation). Repeatable verbs (explain, predict, calibrate, validate) are self-loops that preserve the current state. The Python
implementation tracks this DFA on every Model object; the state machine is
the algebraic backbone of the guard system. Every type in the DAG is reachable from DataFrame through the primitives. The non-terminal types (DataFrame, Partition, PreparedData, Model) form a
chain: each is produced by one primitive and consumed by the next. The
terminal types (Predictions, Metrics, Evidence, Explanation) are all reachable
from Model. Any conforming extension must preserve
this connectivity. The property is reachability (every type can be reached from
the root), not closure in Codd's sense. In the relational algebra, every operator returns a relation, so operators compose freely (Codd-closure: op(𝑅) →𝑅). The grammar has no such property:
predict returns Predictions, not a DataFrame, so split cannot follow predict.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Codd maximized composability; the grammar maximizes
structural safety. A fully closed ML grammar would allow leaky workflows; the
type restrictions that prevent leakage are exactly the restrictions that break free
composability. The tradeoff is intentional. The set of valid workflows the grammar can express is infinite: because the
fit/evaluate loop can repeat any number of times, split →(fit →evaluate)𝑛→
assess is valid for any 𝑛. Each fit and evaluate call draws from the same Partition
produced by split, not from the Metrics output of the previous evaluate (which
is terminal). The cycle exists in the workflow pattern, not in the linear type
chain. The grammar does not cap iteration depth. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 The grammar covers batch supervised learning on a complete dataset
available at split time. Multi-task learning (multiple simultaneous targets), selfsupervised learning (no explicit labels), few-shot learning (𝑁too small to partition meaningfully), federated learning (data never centralized), and online
learning (incremental data arrival) each violate at least one foundational assumption and fall outside the grammar's scope. The grammar generates all
valid workflows within its scope. It makes no claim about valid ML workflows
in general.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
4.3 Rejection criterion The grammar makes specific workflows invalid. Each is rejected by a type check
or a call-time guard: Invalid workflow Why rejected Leakage class assess →assess (same model) Terminal: once per model Class II
prepare(all) →split →fit Prepare must follow split Class I
select_features(X, y) →split No label access before split Class II
→fit
evaluate(data) without prior fit Type error: no Model Class III
fit(test) Guard: test tag not in {train, Class III
valid}
evaluate(test) Guard: test tag reserved for Class III
assess Assess-once (by construction). Constraint 1 specifies: for Model 𝑚,
assess(𝑚, test) is valid if and only if 𝑚.assessed = false. After the first call,
𝑚.assessed ←true. The second call fails the guard. Similarly, prepare(all_data)
before split violates Constraint 2 (the data is unregistered in the provenance
registry, so the guard rejects it), and select_features(X, y) before split violates
Constraint 4. All three are rejected at call time. These are not theorems;
they are consequences of the type guards. The claim is that any conforming
implementation must enforce them.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The Appendix (§ Invalid workflows as
guard failures) walks through each entry in the table above. This grammar is a rejection function: its value lies not in the workflows it enables
but in the workflows it makes impossible to execute within its API boundary. The rejection here is positional, not structural. Chomsky's grammar makes certain strings underivable: the invalid form cannot be generated at all. Codd's
constraints make certain states unstorable: the invalid row cannot exist in the relation. The ML grammar is weaker on both counts: fit(test_data, target)
is a valid Python expression, a valid R call, a valid Julia invocation. The invalid
workflow is representable in the host language. What the grammar rejects is
the transition: test-tagged data entering fit is immediately blocked by a guard A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 that checks a content-addressed partition registry external to the DataFrame. The guard rejects the call, not the code. fit(test_data) is valid Python, but
the registry lookup makes it fail at call time. This is a necessary consequence of
embedding a grammar in a host language that has no native partition type. The
grammar operates at verb entry points, not at the level of the host language's
type system. All four constraints are purely negative: they define the boundary of valid workflows without ranking or recommending within it. The grammar does not specify
which algorithm to choose, how many folds to use, or when to stop iterating;
those are semantics-layer decisions.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The relational
model rejects invalid queries without suggesting good ones. The positive structure of a good ML workflow is the diagnostics and strategy layer's job, not the
grammar's. The property that certain leakage-producing workflows are rejected at call time
— by guard failure or type mismatch, not detected after the fact — is, to my
knowledge, not present in any existing ML framework as a terminal assess-once
constraint. The grammar does not lint completed workflows. It rejects invalid
ones as they are constructed. This claim is bounded to the grammar's own type
system: operations outside the grammar's seven primitives can always produce
leakage that the grammar cannot see. The workflow's structural guarantees are a minimum over its constituent primitives: a violation at split() propagates through all downstream operations,
and no downstream primitive recovers correctness from an upstream violation. This is why the four hard constraints are placed at split and prepare rather
than at assess: the terminal boundary is too late to undo upstream contamination.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
5.1 Triple implementation The grammar is implemented in three languages: Python (mlw on PyPI, imported as import ml; source: github.com/epagogy/ml), R (ml, CRAN submission in progress), and Julia (ML.jl). All three expose the same 7 kernel
primitives with identical type signatures: s = ml.split(df, target="y", seed=42)
model = ml.fit(s.train, "y", algorithm="rf", seed=42)
metrics = ml.evaluate(model, s.valid)
final = ml.assess(model, test=s.test) A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 s <- ml_split(df, target="y", seed=42)
model <- ml_fit(s$train, "y", algorithm="rf", seed=42)
metrics <- ml_evaluate(model, s$valid)
final <- ml_assess(model, test=s$test) s = ML.split(df, "y"; seed=42)
model = ML.fit(s.train, "y"; algorithm="rf", seed=42)
metrics = ML.evaluate(model, s.valid)
final = ML.assess(model; test=s.test)
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The Julia implementation is the grammar's independent portability test. It was
built from the appendix specification without translating Python or R code,
using Julia-native libraries (DecisionTree.jl, XGBoost.jl) and custom implementations (linear models, KNN, Naive Bayes, ElasticNet, SVM) for the algorithm
backends. It reproduces all 7 kernel primitives, all 4 hard constraints, the full
strategy layer (screen, tune, stack, compare, validate), and the diagnostics layer
(profile, check, drift, enough, leak, calibrate, quick): 22 verbs across 12 algorithm families, with 331 tests. The type DAG is identical: SplitResult,
PreparedData, Model, Metrics, and Evidence are distinct named types; the
assess-once guard fires on the second call. fit performs per-fold preparation by
default.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Parity across three languages required no shared code. Each implementation
was built against the grammar specification, not against the other implementations. The verb names change (ml.fit vs ml_fit vs ML.fit). The type
structure is frozen.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
This is the empirical content of the portability claim: a
specification precise enough that three separate implementations converge on
the same behavior without coordinating their internals. Codd (1970) defined the relational model as a mathematical specification (the
later "Codd rules" formalized this as a practical conformance standard). SQL
implementations could be tested against it: does this database system satisfy
the relational algebra? I propose the same spirit for the grammar: does a
given implementation satisfy the type DAG and the four hard constraints? The
analogy is in spirit (a testable conformance standard), not in substance: Codd's
completeness criterion had an external anchor (first-order predicate calculus
expressibility); the seven conditions below are derived from the grammar itself. Concretely, a conforming implementation must: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Produce a Partition from split
2. Require a tagged DataFrame (partition_tag ∈{train, valid, dev})
for fit
3. Require a Model for evaluate and assess
4. Reject a second assess call on the same model
5.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Perform preparation per fold in declarative mode
6. Make label-based feature selection before split fail at the API boundary
(e.g., by rejecting data without split provenance when it enters fit)
7. Return Evidence as a named class that is not implicitly substitutable for
Metrics in the host language; isinstance(result, Evidence) must be
distinguishable from isinstance(result, Metrics) The practical value of the Codd test is decidability: without a grammar, "is this
workflow methodologically correct?" is a matter of opinion. With it, correctness
is enforceable in constant time: seven nodes, four constraints.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The test produces
a binary verdict, not a judgment call. Conditions 1–6 and 7 are verifiable from the API surface: fit rejects unregistered data with a PartitionError whose message directs the user to split
first. The closed-world limitation of prior versions (where untagged data passed
silently) is closed. The Julia implementation serves as a portability test: built
from the specification without translating Python or R source code, it satisfies
all seven conditions using Julia-native libraries.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

5.3 Hierarchy of defaults The grammar operates at two levels of explicitness: Declarative (default): ml.fit(s.train, "y") handles algorithm selection,
preprocessing, and cross-validation internally. The grammar's constraints are
satisfied automatically. This is the entry point for newcomers and the default
mode.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Explicit: Each step can be called independently for full control. ml.prepare(),
ml.fit(), ml.evaluate() as separate operations. The grammar's constraints
still apply: assess is still terminal, preparation is still per-fold. But the user
sees each intermediate type. The default mode does the correct thing. The explicit mode makes the types
visible. 6 Comparison with Existing Frameworks A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 Evaluate/assessTerminal

to the grammar's type DAG. Their types serve pipeline wiring correctness, a
complementary layer to the grammar's methodological validity checks. These
tools are not compared directly because they operate at the execution layer. The grammar operates at the methodological correctness layer. The two layers
compose naturally: a grammar-conforming workflow could run on any of these
execution backends.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Kuhn and Silge (2022) is the closest existing framework. Its recipes enforce
per-fold preprocessing when used inside workflows, preventing Class I leakage
structurally. It deserves explicit credit as the most important prior work. The grammar extends tidymodels' type system one level further. tidymodels
types (recipe, parsnip model, workflow) ensure that preprocessing steps are
applied consistently, which is the right guarantee at the preprocessing boundary. The grammar adds a guarantee at the assessment boundary: a type that marks
a model as terminally assessed, so that repeated test-set evaluation is rejected
at call time. The two systems are complementary.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The grammar's terminal
assess type is the layer tidymodels does not need to provide because its scope
ends before assessment. 6.3 D3M and AutoML grammars DARPA's D3M programme (2018–2022) developed a typed primitive taxonomy
with ~280 annotated components, input/output type annotations, and auto- A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 mated pipeline search. Drori et al. (2019) extends this with formal context-free
grammars over pipeline components. D3M pioneered the idea that ML workflow components should carry typed annotations, and AlphaD3M showed that a formal grammar over those components
could prune the search space effectively. The grammar presented here applies
the same typed-primitive principle to a different question.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
D3M's types answer "what pipelines can I build?" (which components connect to which). The
grammar asks "what workflows are valid?" (which compositions satisfy methodological constraints). The two are complementary: D3M's component taxonomy
could coexist with the grammar's lifecycle constraints, providing search pruning
and correctness checking simultaneously. Binder et al. (2021) is the most formally specified existing system. Its PipeOp
system defines typed inputs and outputs for each pipeline step using graph-based
composition — the strongest prior art for typed ML workflows. mlr3 provides
a composition model (how to build pipelines from components). The grammar
adds a validity model (which compositions satisfy methodological constraints). The two address different layers, and mlr3's engineering could serve as an implementation substrate for the grammar's constraints. The composition algebra is
already there. The rejection criterion would be a layer on top. 6.5 The novelty claim
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The contribution is the terminal assess constraint: the first type-level enforcement of the evaluate/assess boundary in any ML framework, making repeated
test-set assessment rejected at call time in a conforming implementation. The
grammar builds on a chain of prior contributions: Kapoor and Narayanan
(2023) established the leakage taxonomy across 17 fields; LeakageDetector
(Yang, Breck, and Whang 2022) proved that automated detection is feasible;
tidymodels solved per-fold preprocessing; D3M and AlphaD3M showed that
typed primitives can structure ML pipelines; Sculley et al. (2015) named the
structural fragility patterns (entanglement, undeclared consumers, correction
cascades) that persist even in technically correct pipelines. The grammar adds
one layer that this chain had not yet reached: a terminal assess constraint
that rejects the two most damaging leakage classes at the API boundary.

7.4 Cross-validation coverage miscalibration Across 1,903 datasets with 3 algorithms (LR, RF, DT), the nominal 95% confidence interval from 𝑘-fold cross-validation achieves only 55.1% actual coverage A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 (𝑧-based) or 70.4% (𝑡-based). This is consistent with Bengio and Grandvalet
(2004), who proved that no unbiased estimator of CV variance exists. The miscalibration is worst for flexible models (DT: 54.5% 𝑧, 69.3% 𝑡) and best for constrained models (LR: 56.3% 𝑧, 71.9% 𝑡). This implies that evaluate() should
report uncertainty with explicit miscalibration warnings rather than nominal
confidence intervals.

7.8 Implementation stress testing The three implementations have been tested against 2,805 test cases (1,685
Python, 783 R, 337 Julia) covering edge cases, boundary conditions, and
constraint-violation attempts. All three implementations satisfy the 7 Codd
test conditions, including condition 7 (Evidence as a named class distinct
from Metrics): Python uses Evidence(dict) vs Metrics(dict), R uses
ml_evidence vs ml_metrics S3 classes, and Julia uses nominal struct
Evidence vs struct Metrics. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 8.1 What the grammar does not do The grammar prevents structural errors. knowledge
Normalizing binary features Feature-level decisions are semantic This is Chomsky's crack. "Colorless green ideas sleep furiously" is grammatically
valid but semantically nonsense (Chomsky 1957). A workflow that passes all
type checks but produces a poor model for semantic reasons is the ML analogue. A diagnostics layer (check, drift, profile, enough) addresses semantic quality
as a separate concern. Structural validity does not imply numerical reproducibility. fit(data,
seed=42) and fit(data, seed=43) are grammatically identical; they may produce models with different metrics and different conclusions. Reproducibility
requires additional infrastructure beyond fixed seeds (environment versioning,
hardware determinism) that the grammar does not formalize. The grammar
prevents leakage, not non-reproducibility.

text:
This is a
design choice, not an oversight. Validity constraints are enforced at operations,
not at values: a DataFrame is neither valid nor invalid in isolation; it becomes
invalid when passed to fit without a partition tag. The types (DataFrame,
Partition, Model, Metrics) are defined formally in the Appendix and are co-equal
in the specification; they appear secondary in the prose because the actionable
grammar lives on the verbs, not the values flowing between them. Below approximately 𝑁= 30, the grammar's three-way split becomes statistically vacuous. The grammar does not disappear; it contracts: 𝑁 Grammar mode Recommendation < 10 Does not apply Exact tests, Bayesian, case studies
10–29 LOO-CV, one model Report massive uncertainty
30–199 𝑘-fold CV, 1–3 models No screening, no stacking A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 𝑁 Grammar mode Recommendation 200–999 Full grammar feasible Screen, tune, holdout
≥1000 Grammar in its element All strategies available The deeper constraint is not raw 𝑁but the ratio of events to predictors: logistic
regression needs approximately 20 events per predictor (Riley et al. 2019), while
tree ensembles need 3,000+ (van der Ploeg, Austin, and Steyerberg 2014). The
grammar's real contribution at small 𝑁is discipline: separate what you learn
on from what you judge on. That discipline applies at every 𝑁. The mechanism
of separation requires a minimum investment of observations.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The grammar covers tabular binary classification, multiclass classification, and
regression, the setting where the companion study's effect sizes were measured. It does not currently cover deep learning workflows (different type structure: tensors, batches, epochs), unsupervised learning (no target variable, different split
semantics), or structured-data modalities where leakage dynamics differ qualitatively: spatial autocorrelation in ecology (Rosenblatt et al. 2024), tokenization
leakage in NLP, linkage disequilibrium in genomics. The grammar also assumes
a complete dataset at split time: split takes a full DataFrame and partitions
it. Online learning, continual learning, and active learning might violate this
foundational assumption: data arrives incrementally, with no complete rectangle to partition. The grammar is scoped to batch supervised learning; its split
primitive has no defined semantics for streaming inputs. Both evaluate and
assess assume a stationary data-generating process; when the test distribution
has shifted since training (concept drift, covariate shift, population change), the
grammar's type guarantees hold but Evidence no longer measures generalization to the current environment. Temporal evaluation primitives are outside this
grammar's Σ. The grammar's constraints and the companion study's 𝑑-values
do not transfer to these domains. The scope is intentional: tabular supervised
learning is where the train/test boundary is cleanest and all three leakage classes
have been empirically quantified. Extending the grammar to other paradigms
requires analogous empirical baselines first. 8.4 External validation External validation is the missing piece.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The grammar is a specification; the implementation is an approximation of
it. A context-free grammar makes certain strings underivable at the level of
the formal system, not at the level of any particular parser; the same distinction applies here. A user who serializes a model and deserializes it (or calls
copy.deepcopy(model)) can circumvent the assess-once constraint; a user who
mutates a SplitResult's .test attribute can corrupt the assessment boundary. The partition_tag propagation gap was structurally the most significant in
prior versions, and it is an instance of a pattern that recurs whenever a grammar
enforces constraints via metadata attributes in a host language whose standard
operations do not preserve them. Call this tag erasure: typed metadata set
by the grammar is silently discarded by untyped operations in the surrounding environment. In pandas, merge, concat, and groupby strip DataFrame
attributes silently; an attribute-based partition_tag is lost after any standard
DataFrame manipulation between split and fit. The current implementation closes this gap via content-addressed partition identity: split computes a deterministic fingerprint of each output partition (SHA-
256 over hash_pandas_object values) and registers it in a session-scoped provenance registry that maps fingerprints to partition roles and split lineage. Each
verb's guard queries the registry by content fingerprint rather than reading a
metadata attribute. Because the fingerprint is computed from cell values and
column names, not from a metadata attribute, it survives DataFrame operations that preserve both row content and column structure (reindexing, dtype
coercion). Operations that change content or structure (filtering rows, selecting or adding columns, merging, renaming) produce a new fingerprint that is
absent from the registry; the guard rejects the data as unregistered. This is
not a loophole; it is the intended behavior: feature engineering between split
and fit is outside the grammar's seven primitives and is rejected at fit's entry point. The grammar's answer is prepare inside fit, per fold (Constraint
2). The provenance registry additionally stores split lineage (which split call
produced each partition), enabling a cross-verb check at assess time: test data
from a different split than the training data is rejected, closing a second-order
leakage vector (test-set shopping across splits). Tag erasure is not a bug in any
one implementation; it is a structural mismatch between the grammar's typed
requirements and the host environment's untyped operations. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
the architecture the previous paragraph describes: partition provenance stored
in a separate structure that host-language operations do not touch by default. Early SQL implementations violated Codd's relational model routinely: allowing duplicate rows, failing to enforce referential integrity, permitting NULL
semantics that contradicted the specification.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The specification remained correct, and implementations improved over decades. The grammar's enforcement
gaps are probably smaller than early SQL's. The most significant gap, tag
erasure, was resolved via content-addressed partition identity in v1.0.0 of the
Python implementation. The remaining gaps (runtime circumvention, optimization leakage, stopping criterion) are well understood, and the path to closing
them (persistent state, immutable types, affine/linear type system) is established. Three open flanks define the grammar's current ceiling.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
Runtime circumvention:
the assess-once guard is enforced at call time, not compile time; it resets on serialization or copy.deepcopy. Optimization leakage: the grammar is a gate, not
a schedule; indefinite iteration in the evaluate zone is grammatically valid even
when it is methodologically suspect. No stopping criterion: the transition from
iteration to commitment is ungoverned. Together, they mark the boundary between structural validity (what the grammar guarantees) and scientific integrity
(what a rigorous methodology requires). Structural validity is necessary but not
sufficient. A fourth flank, tag erasure, was closed by content-addressed partition
identity (described above): partition provenance stored in a registry external to
the host language's metadata system survives the DataFrame operations that
previously stripped it.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
A fifth gap deserves separate mention: multi-model test-set peeking. The assessonce constraint is per model, not per test set. A practitioner who trains 5
models and assesses each on the same test set has performed model selection
on test data, statistically equivalent to evaluating 5 times. The grammar sees
5 independent, valid assessments. A per-dataset constraint (at most one model
assessed on any given test set) would close this gap but requires global state
across models, breaking the composability that makes the grammar practical. The current design accepts this tradeoff: per-model assess-once catches the most
common abuse (re-running the same model after tweaks) while preserving the
independence of separate modeling efforts. Users comparing multiple models on
the same test set should adjust for multiplicity or use a dedicated comparison
protocol; the grammar does not enforce this.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
8.6 Production and deployment A separate objection holds that the grammar governs training-time structure but
leaves the deployment lifecycle unaddressed: practitioners may call evaluate()
on production batches and treat incoming data as an implicit validation set
without the grammar intervening. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 The objection assumes a distinction between training-time and production-time
that the grammar does not draw. The grammar defines valid workflows as compositions over Σ; that definition is not scoped to a single lifecycle. Production
monitoring decomposes into the same primitives (drift, split, fit, evaluate,
assess) and is therefore a workflow in 𝑊, governed by the same four constraints. If evaluate() on production batches produces leakage, it does so for exactly
the same structural reason that evaluate() on a held-out test set does: data
has been seen before assess() closes the model. The grammar rejects both on
identical grounds. The deployment lifecycle does not require an extension of
the grammar; it is another instantiation of it. 8.7 Feature engineering and the closed-world boundary The grammar's seven primitives do not include feature engineering: no verb for
encoding categoricals, computing lag features, or selecting variables. A natural
objection is that the grammar therefore cannot prevent the most common form
of preprocessing leakage: computing features on the full dataset before splitting. The grammar's answer is that feature engineering belongs inside prepare, which
runs inside fit, per fold (Constraint 2). This is not a workaround; it is the
structurally correct position.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
In temporal cross-validation, features derived from
the data (rolling averages, lag variables, target encodings) must be computed
using only the training data available at each fold's time cutoff. Computing
them before the split uses future data to construct past features, a temporal
leakage that inflates performance estimates. The same argument applies to
standard cross-validation: target-encoding before the split uses test labels to
construct training features. In computationally intensive settings with many
features or large datasets, per-fold preparation can become a bottleneck. A
future grammar extension may introduce a safe precomputation primitive that
runs before split but is restricted to leakage-free transformations (e.g., static
type conversions, column selection) while routing target-dependent operations
through the per-fold path. The grammar specifies this position structurally: fit requires data that has
passed through split. The implementation enforces this by rejecting data without split provenance: not just data with a known-wrong tag, but any data that
has not been registered by split. This forces all feature engineering through
prepare per fold: there is no path from raw data to fit that bypasses split.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
This closes what appeared to be a closed-world limitation. The grammar does
not need to detect pre-split feature engineering; it makes the pre-split path
structurally inaccessible. The prepare verb is deliberately opaque, a monolith
that handles encoding, scaling, imputation, and derived features internally. Decomposing prepare into typed sub-operations (encode, scale, impute, derive,
select) is a natural extension that would make each transformation step visible
while preserving the per-fold constraint. That decomposition is left for future
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 work; the current grammar's guarantee is that prepare runs per fold, and no
unregistered data can bypass it. 8.8 Enforcement philosophy The grammar's four constraints admit two enforcement strategies. A gate rejects
invalid input at every entry point: fit refuses data without split provenance,
making the unsplit path fail at entry. A funnel makes the correct path the path
of least resistance without blocking the incorrect one. The distinction matters
because Class II and III protection is downstream of split: a user who never
splits gets no partition tags, and the guards at evaluate and assess never fire. Advisory protection that only catches mistakes by users who already do the
right thing is circular. The implementation therefore enforces split provenance structurally: fit,
evaluate, assess, and validate all reject data that has not passed through
split. Every guard checks the content-addressed registry; data with no
fingerprint match (unregistered) is rejected with a message directing the user
to split first. This closes the dependency: the guards that prevent Class II
and III leakage (𝑑𝑧= 0.53–1.11) are activated by the same mechanism that
prevents Class I (|𝑑| < 0.1). The cost is real: the simple path (ml.fit(df,
"y") without a prior split call) no longer works by default. A configuration
switch (config(guards="off")) disables all guards for exploration, education,
and rapid prototyping where structural enforcement is unnecessary. This is
an explicit exit from the grammar's jurisdiction, analogous to Rust's unsafe
blocks, which do not weaken the type system but mark where the programmer
has left it.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The three implementations enforce provenance through different mechanisms: Python uses content-addressed fingerprinting (SHA-256 over cell
values, session-scoped registry), R uses DataFrame attributes (attr(data,
"_ml_partition")), and Julia uses DataFrames.metadata(). The mechanisms currently differ in robustness to host-language operations (fingerprinting
survives copy() and reindexing; attributes and metadata are lost on reconstruction). The R and Julia implementations are expected to converge on
content-addressed fingerprinting before release. The grammatical semantics
are already identical across all three: every verb rejects data without split
provenance when guards are active. The grammar is language-agnostic; the
enforcement adapts to each host's idioms.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
8.9 Implementation variance Two conforming implementations of the same algorithm can produce different
predictive surfaces. In cross-engine experiments, implementations of SVM using different solvers produced 𝑅2 values of 0.964 and 0.917 on identical data
with identical hyperparameters. The grammar guarantees type-correctness; it A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 does not guarantee behavioral equivalence across engines. This is a genuine
expressiveness limitation that has to be acknowledged.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
8.10 Data flow and operational flow The type DAG models data flow: which types are produced and consumed by
each primitive. Strategy verbs (tune, screen, stack) have implicit operational
semantics that the type DAG does not capture. tune calls fit and evaluate
internally; its type signature tune :: Model × HyperparameterSpace →
TuningResult hides this orchestration. The grammar specifies what types flow
between operations but not which operations trigger which. For correctness
checking this is sufficient: the type guard at each primitive boundary is what
prevents leakage, not the order in which strategies invoke primitives internally. The composition graph reveals that the seven primitives are not structural peers.
evaluate is a hub: six verbs compose through it internally (assess, validate,
compare, screen, shelf, and tune all call evaluate on their data). This hub
structure has a practical consequence for guard design: evaluate carries the
tightest partition constraint (rejecting test-tagged data), yet every finalization
verb must route test data through it. The implementation resolves this via
a trusted/untrusted calling context — public calls enforce the guard; internal
composition bypasses it. But the type DAG does not represent this distinction.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
A complete account of ML workflow structure would distinguish the data flow
graph from the control flow graph; the current grammar covers the former. The control-flow gap is most visible in the strategy verbs' implicit temporal
shapes: tune is iterative (a loop that fits and evaluates repeatedly until convergence), screen is parallel (multiple algorithms evaluated simultaneously against
the same Partition), and stack is sequential-then-meta (base models trained independently in the first pass, then a meta-learner trained on their out-of-fold
predictions in the second). These temporal shapes are nowhere in the type signatures; they emerge from the strategy definitions, not from the type DAG. A
practitioner reading only the type DAG cannot infer that tune is a loop and
screen is a fan-out.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The grammar covers data flow; a complete account would
also formalize control flow. That extension is left for future work. 8.11 Structural severity and optimization leakage The grammar is a gate, not a schedule: it controls which data flows to which
primitive but does not govern how many times the iterate zone runs or when
the transition to assess is triggered. The grammar enforces structural severity: assess uses held-out data that has
not participated in any prior training or evaluation call.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
This closes data leakage. It does not close optimization leakage: a practitioner who runs screen() with
11 algorithms, tune() with 500 hyperparameter configurations, and then calls
assess() has made hundreds of model selection decisions on the validation set. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 The grammar considers this valid. Statistically, the final assess result is biased
upward because the model was selected to look good on validation data. The
grammar prevents test-set leakage but does not prevent validation-set overfitting
through repeated evaluation. This is the dominant failure mode in modern ML
practice and the grammar's most significant limitation. Mayo (1996) requires that a hypothesis passes a severe test only if it would
probably have failed the test were it false.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The grammar satisfies the structural
condition: the assessor is held-out data independent of training. It does not
enforce the epistemic condition: the model could have been tuned until evaluation looked good, in which case a weak model may still pass assess on a
favorable split. The evaluation metric was optimized rather than simply measured, a familiar pattern in model selection that the grammar does not currently
address. Nested cross-validation partially addresses this: the outer loop provides an unbiased estimate of generalization performance while the inner loop handles model
selection. The grammar is compatible with nested CV — it is a valid workflow
within the grammar's scope; it does not require it. For high-stakes applications, nested CV or a pre-registered stopping criterion (how many evaluation
iterations are permitted, or what convergence condition triggers assess) would
close the optimization leakage gap. The grammar specifies the type boundary;
it does not specify the transition condition. That specification is left to the
practitioner.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
A typed gate state — a permission sort that transitions GateState ∈{Locked,
Unlocked} when assess is first called — would make the temporal boundary
explicit in the type system but would not close this gap. A gate records when
access was granted, not whether access was earned. The distinction matters: a
model that passed only because the practitioner iterated evaluation until metrics
looked favorable would still unlock the gate. Closing both gaps simultaneously
requires a more expressive type: a gate that cannot open unless a pre-registered
stopping criterion has been satisfied. That system — typed permission conditioned on pre-registered termination — is the minimal extension that would
enforce both structural severity and epistemic legitimacy jointly. The current
grammar enforces one; achieving the other is left to pre-registration protocol,
not type enforcement. The grammar of ML workflows shifts where the work happens.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The evaluate/assess boundary is not a rule to follow; it is a constraint the implementation
enforces. The types remember so you don't have to. Seven primitives, four constraints, three separate implementations, 2,805 tests,
and a companion study across 2,047 experimental instances supplemented by
3,759 𝑛-scaling, seed-stability, and coverage instances (Roth 2026): the gram- A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
The leakage classes it rejects produce large standardized effects
(𝑑𝑧= 0.93–1.11; raw AUC inflation +0.02–0.09 points) that persist across all
tested sample sizes (𝑛= 50–2,000) with a positive asymptotic floor; the class it
tolerates does not (|𝑑| < 0.1). Three testable predictions: two confirmed, one
falsified. The grammar is falsifiable and survives the test. The type DAG and four constraints are frozen: they define what a conforming
implementation must satisfy. The verb names, function signatures, and language binding are the changeable surface. The Julia implementation — built
from the specification without translating Python or R source, reproducing all
7 primitives, 4 constraints, and 22 verbs across 12 algorithm families — demonstrates that the specification is sufficient for independent reimplementation. The
open empirical question is whether the grammar reduces leakage errors in practice. The test is straightforward: a randomized study comparing leakage rates
in student or researcher code written with the grammar versus without it. That
test would require a reference implementation whose conformance to the type
DAG and seven Codd conditions is certified, so that "written with the grammar"
means the same thing across conditions. Three certified implementations now
exist. The grammar makes the hypothesis precise enough to run that test. Appendix: Formal Specification The grammar is a typed specification over eight types. Each primitive has
a type signature, a guard (precondition checked at call time), and an effect
(state mutation after call). Two types are terminal (their values feed no further
primitive):

title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows

text:
A workflow is invalid if any step fails a type check or a guard. The four hard
constraints are guards in this sense: not rules a user must remember, but checks
that reject invalid workflows at call time. Note on evaluate guard. The guard on evaluate rejects test-tagged data;
it accepts train and valid partitions. Evaluating on training data is legitimate
(train-vs-valid score comparison for overfitting diagnosis). The guard prevents
test data from entering the iterate cycle: if evaluate accepted test data, the
practitioner could iterate on test-set feedback, which is structurally equivalent
to training on the test set. The assess primitive is the only path for test
data. This is a design decision: evaluate is the practice exam (repeatable, on
validation data); assess is the final exam (terminal, on test data). Note on branching strategies. This definition covers linear workflows. Branching strategies (screen, tune, stack) involve parallel or iterated applications of the same primitives; their validity follows from applying the same type
signatures and guards to each branch independently. A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows Roth, 2026 Note on domain specializations. split admits domain specializations
(split_temporal, split_group) that share its type signature but carry
additional guards specific to a scientific domain. This is a representational
mechanism, not a primitive extension: the guard set 𝐺(split) is parameterized
by domain, but the type signature and position in the DAG are invariant. A
grammar for time series forecasting inherits all 7 primitives and adds temporal
guards to split; the kernel is unchanged.
title: A Grammar of Machine Learning Workflows
